retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The impression compound has low thermal conductivity. Therefore, one should check the softening of the material during manipulation and complete material setting before removing it from the oral cavity. Glass transition temperature: This is when amorphous substances start to lose their hardness, for the impression compound is 39° Celsius. Fusion temperature: The impression compound reaches the fusion temperature at 43.5° Celsius. Above this temperature, the impression compound remains plastic and can be utilized. Viscosity: The impression compound is highly viscous; therefore, impressions are mucocompressive in nature. It gives an extended impression but lacks detail. 7. Advantages: It can be reused in the same patient until a good impression is made. It can compensate for a short impression tray because it does not sag under its weight. It can be electroplated to form dies. It can be added or removed until a good impression is made. 8. Disadvantages:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 5. Manipulation: Type 1 sheet impression compound is softened in a water bath with warm water and is placed in a stock tray to copy edentulous ridges. The water bath temperature is kept at around 55-60° Celsius, above the impression compound's fusion temperature. [6] The impression compound is a mucocompressive material, meaning that it impresses the mucosa when subjected to compression. These impressions must be corrected with a second impression made in a custom impression tray fabricated on the primary cast of the patient. The type 1 stick impression compound is softened over a flame and placed on the borders of the custom impression tray. [7] The type 2 impression compound is used to create an impression tray for making a wash impression. 6. Properties:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wettability of the impression material determines its ability to record fine details, which depends on its viscosity. The more the impression material wets the tissues, the more it comes in close contact with them and captures fine details. The more viscous materials have limited flow and very few wetting features. [4] In fixed dental prostheses, an impression is expected to reproduce 20 to 70 microns, and 100 to 150 microns in removable prosthodontics. According to international standards, the impression material should record a line of 0.02 mm width or less, which is less than the width of a human hair. [5] Impression Materials Classification: 1. Based on use: For primary impressions, eg, alginate, impression compound, and elastomeric putty. For secondary impressions, eg, light body elastomers and zinc oxide eugenol. 2. Based on the setting reaction:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrophilic, meaning better impressions in the presence of saliva. Agar has been used for making subgingival crown preparation impressions when it is not easy to keep the area dry. Dimensional stability: As this is a hydrocolloid (water-based), it may lead to deformation due to imbibition (absorption of water ) or syneresis (exudation of fluids) if the impression is not poured immediately. It has an optimum flow to record fine details of hard and soft tissues. Compatibility issues with gypsum arise because borax interferes with gypsum setting; therefore, a gypsum hardener is added. 5. Advantages: Reversible Affordable Nontoxic, odorless, and nonstaining 6. Disadvantages Requires purchasing a water bath Low tear strength Dimensional instability Infection control unfriendly Elastomeric Impression Materials

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Composition: The base contains hydroxy-terminated polydimethylsiloxane, which forms a polymer, and fillers that control viscosity. The accelerator contains tetraethyl silicate and tin octoate (catalyst) [25] 4. Setting reaction: Condensation polymerization reaction occurs with the release of alcohol as a byproduct that evaporates as it is formed. 5. Advantages: Good elastic recovery Pleasant odor and taste Accurate reproduction 6. Disadvantages: Dimensional deformation over time Hydrophobic Dimensional stability is affected due to reaction byproducts [25] Delivery Systems of Elastomers

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrophilic Accurate and high-dimensional stability enables the delay of casting and allows for multiple pourings. Good elastic recovery Good compatibility with gypsum Good shelf life It can be used as a single-phase material or with a syringe tray technique 6. Disadvantage: The most rigid of all the elastomers makes it hard to remove (newer polyethers are slightly more flexible) May cause allergy due to sulfonate acid esters The impression should not be immersed in disinfectant for more than 10 minutes since it's hydrophilic. [22] [23] Since it absorbs water, it should not be kept with alginate impressions. Polysulfide Polysulfides are the first elastomers to be introduced. Although messy to manipulate, it is useful when a long working time is needed. Polysulfides are not recommended except for complete dentures. 1. Consistencies: light or heavy body 2. Mode of supply: It is available in a 2-paste system: base and accelerator.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Dustless alginates: manufactured to prevent inhalation of alginate particles, which may cause respiratory problems. The alginate particles are coated with glycol or glycerine to make them denser. Chromatic alginates: change colors during manipulation to guide mixing time and setting time. In the form of sol or paste (containing water) and a reactor (plaster of Paris) separately. Agar Agar is another hydrophilic colloid impression material like alginate, but it is reversible. It is the first elastic impression material to be used in dentistry. 1. Composition: Chemically, agar is a sulfuric ester of the galactan complex, and agar is available as a tray or syringe material. Agar (gelling agent) Borax (strengthener) Potassium sulfate (gypsum hardener) Alkyl benzoate (preservatives) Water (reaction medium > 80%) Fillers, flavoring, and coloring agents

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Impression compound is a thermoplastic, rigid, and reversible impression material (meaning it can be reused in the same patient if an impression needs to be repeated). It is also known as a modeling compound. See Image. Impression Compound. 1. Types of impression compound Type 1: Low-fusing impression compound. Available as a sheet, rope, and stick. The sheet material impresses edentulous arches, and the stick form is used for border molding. Type 2: High-fusing impression compound, also known as tray compound. It is used for making impression trays. 2. Composition of impression compound Rosin 30 parts Resin 30 parts Carnauba wax 10 parts Stearic acid 5parts (gives plasticity) Talc 75parts (provides hardness) Coloring agents 3. Setting reaction: a thermoplastic material that sets by physical reaction only. 4. Instruments and armamentarium: water bath, gauge piece, burner, scalpel handle, and blade to modify the impression.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Wash impressions (secondary impression) in complete denture fabrication and dual impression in distal extension partial dentures. Bite registration and interocclusal records. [12] 6. Contraindications: It is a brittle material; it cannot impress dentulous mouths or severe undercut areas. It should not be used in patients who are allergic to eugenol. 7. Advantages: Economical and easy to use Good flow helps in recording fine details Dimensionally stable 8. Disadvantages: It cannot be used in stock trays; custom trays are mandatory Fragile Can produce a burning sensation of the mucosa Setting time varies with temperature and humidity 9. Properties: Rigid Low viscosity Good surface details in thin section (0.5mm) Dimensionally stable (0.1% contraction) Compatible with gypsum products Alginate

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is a hydrocolloid widely used as an impression material in dentistry. It is derived from alginic acid, present in brown algae. It is an irreversible and elastic impression material. [13] See Image. Alginate, Impression Making. 1. Composition: Sodium or potassium alginate (15%) - dissolves in water and forms a viscous sol Calcium sulfate (16%) - reactor Diatomaceous earth (60%) - filler Zinc oxide (4%) - filler Potassium titanium fluoride (3%) - accelerator Sodium phosphate (2%) - retarder Glycol (in dust-free alginates) Pigments and flavoring agents 2. Indications: Diagnostic casts for treatment planning Orthodontic study models Impressions to fabricate partial and complete dentures, and temporomandibular disorder appliances For duplicating models 3. Instruments and armamentarium: flexible rubber bowl, curved spatula, measuring scoop, perforated impression tray, alginate powder, and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE) impression material is an irreversible nonelastic material used as a final impression (wash impression) in complete denture fabrication. It records the tissues in the undistorted state. [9] [10] 1. Composition: ZOE impression material is available in 2 collapsible tubes; 2 contain the base and the other the catalyst (see Image . Zinc Oxide Eugenol). The base contains ZnO 87%, vegetable or mineral oils 13%. The catalyst contains eugenol 12-15%, rosin 50%, fillers 20%, resinous balsam 10%, accelerator, and color pigments 5%. *Noneugenol pastes (for patients who are allergic to eugenol) contain carboxylic acids. 2. Instruments and armamentarium: paper pad or glass slab and broad-end spatula. 3. Manipulation: equal lengths are squeezed out from both tubes on a paper pad or a glass slab and mixed with a broad-end spatula until they are mixed to an even color.

retrieved_docs.content: Reversible (physical): impression compound, impression waxes, agar. Irreversible (chemical): alginate, impression plaster, elastomeric impression materials. 3. Based on the state of the impression material after setting in the oral cavity: Rigid: impression compound, zinc oxide eugenol, impression plaster, impression waxes. Elastic: alginate, agar, polysulfides, addition and condensation silicones, polyether. 4. Based on compression of underlying tissues: Mucostatic: impression plaster, light body elastomers. Mucocompressive: impression compound, putty, and elastomers. 5. Based on consistencies: Light body Medium body Heavy body Putty

retrieved_docs.content: 2. Manipulation: It requires hydrocolloid conditioner equipment with 3 compartments to heat, store, and temper the material, and special impression trays that can circulate water. First, the material is boiled in the first compartment and changed to a sol from a gel state. In the second compartment, the material is stored at 65° Celsius and used when needed. The agar is brought to 45° Celsius in the tempering section to impress oral tissues comfortably. The water circulates through the tubing and converts the sol into a gel state. 3. Indications: The agar has been used in dentistry for cast duplication and impression making. Its use is now limited as it requires a complicated setup. [18] 4. Properties:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine is incorporated into a variety of topical formulations with differing functional roles. In the product “Niacinamide 2.4 g in 120 mL / Panthenol 0.12 g in 120 mL / Adenosine 0.04 g in 120 mL TOPICAL LIQUID [VITA VOLU 101 ESSENCE]”, adenosine is listed as an active ingredient, indicating it contributes directly to the therapeutic or cosmetic effect of the lotion. Similarly, in “Niacinamide 3.6 g in 180 mL / Adenosine 0.07 g in 180 mL TOPICAL LIQUID [VITA VOLU VOLUME BOOSTER]”, adenosine is identified as an active moiety, reinforcing its role as a key pharmacologically active component. Conversely, adenosine appears as an inactive ingredient in several sunscreen‑type creams, such as the “TITANIUM DIOXIDE 1.0752 g in 12 g / OCTINOXATE 0.84 g in 12 g / OCTISALATE 0.36 g in 12 g TOPICAL CREAM [SNP Animal Panda Luminous Cover Cushion]”, the “TITANIUM DIOXIDE 1.245 g in 15 g / OCTINOXATE 1.05 g in 15 g / OCTISALATE 0.45 g in 15 g TOPICAL CREAM [OSEQUE HIGH END TOUCH WATERFIT COVER CUSHION Natural Beige]”, and the “TITANIUM DIOXIDE 1.4592 g in 12 g / OCTINOXATE 0.84 g in 12 g / OCTISALATE 0.36 g in 12 g TOPICAL CREAM [SNP Animal Tiger Long‑lasting Cover Cushion]”. In these formulations, adenosine does not contribute to the primary active effect but is present as a filler or stabilizer. Additionally, adenosine is linked to salicylic acid 10 mg/mL in a topical lotion, where salicylic acid is designated as an inactive ingredient relative to adenosine, suggesting that adenosine may be the principal active component while salicylic acid serves a supporting role. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that adenosine can function either as a primary active agent or as an ancillary component depending on the specific product composition, highlighting the importance of understanding ingredient roles for accurate assessment of therapeutic and cosmetic efficacy.

Conversely, lysine is repeatedly identified as an inactive ingredient in numerous topical and dermatologic products. It is present in dimethicone‑based preparations such as Dimethicone 17.5 µg / 1 mg topical liquid and Dimethicone 50 µg / 1 mg topical cream, as well as in higher‑strength dimethicone creams (17.5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL). Cosmetic liquids containing niacinamide, panthenol and adenosine—VITA VOLU 101 ESSENCE and VITA VOLU VOLUME BOOSTER—also list lysine as an inactive component. Several mineral‑oil‑free cover cushions that combine titanium dioxide, octinoxate and octisalate (SNP Animal Panda Luminous Cover Cushion, OSEQUE HIGH END TOUCH WATERFIT COVER CUSHION Natural Beige, and SNP Animal Tiger Long‑lasting Cover Cushion) incorporate lysine as an excipient. Additional products that contain lysine as an inactive ingredient include a benzalkonium chloride 1.3

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Arginine is an amino acid that exists in a salt form known as arginine hydrochloride. Arginine hydrochloride is employed as an inactive ingredient in the formulation of a topical cream containing 0.5 mg/mL capsaicin, serving as an excipient that helps stabilize the product without contributing therapeutic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 298
retrieved_docs.content: Lysine (referred to as “lysin” in the source data) is incorporated in a wide variety of pharmaceutical and cosmetic formulations, serving either as an active moiety or as an inactive ingredient. In injectable solutions such as Trophamine 6 % (which contains a detailed amino‑acid profile), lysine is listed as an active moiety, indicating a therapeutic role in the preparation. Numerous topical products list lysine as an inactive component, including dimethicone‑based creams and liquids (e.g., 17.5 µg/1 mg and 50 µg/1 mg dimethicone formulations), titanium‑dioxide–containing cushions (SNP Animal Panda Luminous Cover Cushion, OSEQUE HIGH END TOUCH WATERFIT COVER CUSHION, SNP Animal Tiger Long‑lasting Cover Cushion), and niacinamide/adenosine skin‑care essences (VITA VOLU 101 ESSENCE and VITA VOLU VOLUME BOOSTER). Lysine also appears as an inactive ingredient in benzalkonium chloride topical solution, as well as in products that combine other actives such as miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL cream and salicylic acid 10 mg/mL lotion. Additional cosmetic formulations that feature dimethicone (both 17.5 µg/mL and 50 µg/mL concentrations) and various UV filters (octinoxate, octisalate) similarly include lysine among their inactive constituents. Beyond these formulations, lysine‑derived compounds such as chenodeoxycholyl‑nitrobenzoxadiazolyl‑lysine and deoxycholyl‑L‑lysyl‑methylester are explicitly mapped to lysine, underscoring their structural relationship to the amino acid. Collectively, these relationships illustrate lysine’s versatile presence across injectable, topical, and cosmetic products, where it may contribute to formulation stability, skin‑care benefits, or therapeutic activity depending on the specific product context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2017
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc products, emollients and protectives are classified as a type of emollient and protective agent and are directly associated with zinc (zinek), indicating that these formulations incorporate zinc as a key component in their skin‑protective and moisturizing properties.
